dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 for a 9-year-old with convulsions reported at --Address Redacted-- in Richmond.
Hi, I am Taylor, an EMT with the rescue squad. We were called for a seizure with status epilepticus. Are there family members here with the patient?
Yes, it's her son, the patient.
Is he aware of his surroundings at all?
No, his eyes are closed and have not opened since the last seizure.
No motor response. Trying to elicit a response now.
Airway appears patent. Can someone stay with him during the transfer to the stretcher?
Breathing is irregular in both rate and depth, with no signs of retractions or increased effort.
Strong, equal radial and pedal pulses. Vital signs are 117/69 mmHg, with a heart rate of 99 and respiratory rate of 12.
Oxygen saturation is 98% in room air, but I will put him on 4LPM nasal cannula to improve that.
EKG shows normal sinus rhythm.
Blood glucose is 103 mg/dL.
Capnography shows irregular respirations and elevated ETCO2 levels between 60-70 mmHg, suggesting hypercarbia.
Could someone share the events leading up to the seizure?
He was acting normal. He left to go to the park around 3:30 with friends.
Then my daughter called at 3:35. She said he had slurred speech and seemed confused before driving him.
While driving, he had a full seizure that lasted about one minute. Firefighters arrived and witnessed another large seizure without him regaining awareness.
He has not returned to a normal state since and remains unresponsive.
Has he had any medical issues or a history of seizures?
No. This is the first time. He is healthy with no known medical concerns.
When was the last thing he had to eat or drink?
I think it was around 1500, maybe a sandwich and juice.
On a scale from 0 to 10, with 10 as the worst pain, would you say the patient is experiencing pain right now?
He's unconscious throughout, so I don't think he's in pain.
Performing Glasgow Coma Scale now. Eye Opening--No response. Verbal--No response. Motor--No response. Total GCS score is 3.
Checking the eyes-pupils are 3mm, sluggish, and locked to the right.
Breath sounds are clear and equal bilaterally. Normal chest rise and fall.
Radial and pedal pulses are strong. No swelling or edema. No signs of injury in arms and legs.
Initiating four-lead ECG monitoring.
Capnography is active. Breathing appears irregular with hypercarbia noted. ETCO2 between 60-70 mmHg.
Starting IV line in the right arm.
Administering 2.5mg of midazolam IM to help control the ongoing seizure activity.
Injection delivered in the left deltoid at 16:09.
No improvement in mental status or seizure control after the midazolam.
We are following Medical - Seizure (Protocol 9-12) based on the clinical findings and history.
Current vitals are BP 118/69, respiratory rate 12, heart rate 77.
Oxygen saturation remains at 98%, and the EKG still shows normal sinus rhythm.
The glucose reading is now at 0-probably due to a machine error.
Contacted VCU Children's Hospital with a pre-arrival report for pediatric ED Room 2.
Transport is secure in semi-fowler's position with shoulder and lap belts. Oxygen ongoing at 4LPM nasal cannula to maintain saturation above 94%.
IV line patent and functioning. Midazolam given at 16:09.
Re-evaluating capnography, ETCO2 holds steady around 65 mmHg.
Four-lead EKG still shows normal sinus rhythm-no changes from initial reading.
Retesting blood glucose now to confirm the initial reading.
Glucose is still showing 0-will note as a possible device error in the report.
Did anyone notice any possible toxins or recent falls before the seizure?
No, his friends and sister report nothing happened. No recent illness, fever, or NVD reported.
Skin is warm, dry, with normal color and less than 2 seconds for cap refill.
No signs of trauma or deformity. Neck veins unremarkable.

dialogue.role: nose, mouth and throat exam; Partner
dialogue.utterance: No bleeding or drainage from ears or nose. No foreign body.
No signs of trauma to chest or abdomen. No masses noted.
Abdomen soft with no rigidity or tenderness at this time.
Retesting with alternative site for accuracy.
Still shows 0. Record noted as suspected machine failure in documentation.
No signs of pain-GCS indicates no perceived sensation or response to pain at this time.
No current medications-family states none are taken regularly.
No known drug allergies noted in family history.
Accounts consistent: normal behavior, then call at 3:35. Later seizure while being driven.
Oxygen via nasal cannula at 4LPM, and working well.
Retake BP-118/69, radial-77. Stable vitals.
Re-asking about family history or prior medications.
Still no prior health issues, medications, or allergies.
Oxygen saturation remains at 98%, stable with current flow and administration.
Destination confirmed as VCU ED-pediatric room 2.
EKG shows sinus rhythm with no dysrhythmia present.
Continuing 4LPM O2 and monitoring vitals en route.
Patient remains unresponsive.
Scooping personal information now before arriving at hospital.
No other pertinent history to note-family denies any recent illnesses or injuries.
Patient: male, 9 years old, 30 kg.
Patient leave record to hospital upon arrival with all findings.
Rechecking protocol implementation for en route care.
All interventions remain active during transport.
Vital signs still stable. BP 118/69, heart rate 77, respirations 12, O2 98%.
GCS remains at 3-no improvement in neurological condition.
Transport conditions appear stable and safe per protocol.
Reconfirming glucose is zero, noted due to suspected machine error and no intervention required.
Hospital notified of all findings and interventions. Pre-arrival confirmed.
EKG waveform shows sinus rhythm with no changes present.
No new interventions-midazolam once, IV line functioning.
COMPLETE-CV monitoring and Capnography.
Patient shows no signs of pain. Score at zero per GCS and clinical response.
Rescue straps and shoulder/lap restraints in place.
Initiating non-urgent cardiac monitoring for trip duration.
O2<Nasal Cannula is in place with proper flow.
Patient is on semi-fowler's, stable, and non-repositioned in travel.
All protocol steps followed as per protocol 9-12.
No changes or additions to the protocol during assessment.
All interventions remain active during transport.
Vital signs still stable. BP 118/69, heart rate 77, respirations 12, O2 98%.
No transport instability reported. En route to destination.
No pain rating possible. Patient status is unresponsive and non-verbal.
Transport progressing without incident.
All papers, equipment, and the patient移交 prepared for hospital care.
Back on designated route after brief delay for equipment maintenance.
Delay due to equipment switch and resource availability-no patient compromise.
Found no deviation from protocol-maintained strict adherence.
Rechecking heart rate-still 77 and regular.
Ongoing assessments for life support and possible changes in status.
Monitoring still active and stable.
Transport to VCU Children's ED Room 2 confirmed-arrival estimated within seven minutes.
Glucose remains 0-documentation updated as machine error.
Transport progressing without incident.
Handoff completed with all pertinent information, monitoring data, IV, and midazolam administration transferred to ED staff.
All tools and medications documented and transferred.
All vitals, procedures, and medications handed to ED staff verbally and in paper.
Transport complete with safe patient delivery to destination.
Patient removed from stretcher-handoff full.
No abnormalities-adherence full.
